data.title: FXI: Chinese Stocks Are Far From A Bubble And Doomsayers May Be Disappointed
data.description:
I wrote an article in May titled "Why This Could be a Great Time to Buy China." After the recent stimulus surge and fade, the index iShares China Large-Cap ETF (NYSEARCA:FXI) is still up 20%. I believe the opportunity is still just beginning despite many media outlets rushing to pour water on the upside potential.

The market's obsession with stimulus is unhealthy

The global financial crisis in 2007-2008 has created a market environment obsessed with stimulus measures. The market narrative every day is based on what the Federal Reserve will or won't do.

In reality, the Federal Reserve, like other central banks, is focused on bringing rates lower as inflation continues to fall. At this point, investors should be looking for ideas that will benefit, or suffer, from the return to lower rates. Do you need to watch every inflation print to know that Net Interest Income (NII) is now on a path that could hurt investment banks?

Likewise in China, investors and the media are ignoring the bigger picture as they focus on the market's interpretation of what the ideal size of stimulus is.

The Federal Reserve's interest cut in September was immediately followed by an interest rate cut from the People's Bank of China ((PBoC)). Chinese policymakers are keen to follow the Fed's actions and the bank's governor, Pan Gongsheng, signaled another 25-50 basis point cut by year-end.

Many market watchers are now obsessed with the size of the latest stimulus measures and are missing the point that Chinese policymakers have drawn a line in the sand for future action. The PBoC is smart enough to know not to expend a huge amount of its tools when there is no financial panic.

Are investors backing the wrong horse in U.S. stocks?

Another reality we have to consider is the skewed valuations between U.S. and Chinese stocks. I believe the market is making an error here.

On a simple visual basis, the Chinese index of the top 50 shares was treading very low levels that are still 33% lower than the 2021 highs. In contrast, the S&P 500 is up around 22% over that period.

The chart above also makes 2015 bubble comparisons look ridiculous.

A recent analysis from JPMorgan analysts said that China's stimulus measures were a "major disappointment" as they missed the market's expectations for a 2 trillion RMB stimulus.

The argument from JPMorgan, which is underweight on Chinese equities, is that stimulus has not been targeted toward personal consumption and deflation.

The United States is currently growing its economy at around 3% on an annualized basis. China's economy has its issues and analysts are worried they may not meet the 5% annual growth target. But the economy is still far ahead of Europe, which is closer to recession with very weak growth.

Despite some recent gloom, there are bright spots emerging with a four-month-high in retail sales for September. There is underlying strength in spending, but a lot of that goes overseas. Chinese overseas luxury spending was 32% higher in May than in 2019. A focus on weak consumer confidence may also be improved by the government's recent stimulus and its lowering of interest rates.

Chinese stocks are being unfairly punished

Warren Buffett often uses the term "Mr Market" to discuss value opportunities. The Chinese situation is a prime example of investors' focus on the overall market, ignoring company-specific performance.

If we take e-commerce giant Alibaba (BABA) as an example, the company's revenue on a trailing twelve-month basis is down -3% from the 2021 high in the stock market. Despite this small downturn, the company's stock was down almost -80% from that period, and is still down around -68%.

Looking at other elements of the company's financials, we can see that operating income is actually up 10% over the same period.

It is this type of disconnect that has brought hedge fund investors into Chinese stocks. Ray Dalio's Bridgewater Associates said the latest stimulus measures were "a significant difference," from previous attempts to support the market.

In my view, the valuation risk is more in U.S. stocks than in Chinese. Investors of companies in the United States have been pricing in the Federal Reserves interest rate cuts well ahead of the event. There is also a large AI premium being added to stocks that are continually hitting all-time highs at a time of global geopolitical tensions and questions over AI demand.

We can also look at the valuation comparison between Alibaba and its U.S. e-commerce rival Amazon (AMZN). With a forward Enterprise Value/Sales ratio of 3.21x, it is more than 100% higher than Alibaba's 1.55x.

All things considered, I think that some economic woes in China are punishing its stocks, and that is also being driven by the effects of investor sentiment on capital flows. I noted in my previous article that investment flows, particularly from overseas investors, have been volatile. A surge into May receded again, but the recent stimulus measures could see that return.

The PBoC's latest actions led to a $5.2 billion inflow for the week ended October 4, after an average weekly outflow of $83 million for the year and an average weekly outflow of $27 million last year. In my view, Chinese stocks are nowhere near a bubble when consumer confidence is strained and foreign investors have been non-existent. China's 2015 market rally was also driven by an investor frenzy, and that could return in the future if the market recovers.

Risks to the investment thesis

Risks to the investment thesis can be reduced if investors are investing over a longer timeframe. China's economy is still growing at a strong level, and the latest stimulus measures may be a spark that can bring consumer spending and confidence back. Problems in the property sector cannot be fixed overnight, but policymakers are showing a commitment to stabilize the market.

The risks in the short term are from the investment flows into stocks. The $5.2 billion weekly inflow was a step in the right direction, but the gloomy market sentiment around the size of the stimulus may keep buying subdued. The economy will have to show signs of recovery to create more lasting support.

There is also some risk from the upcoming U.S. election, where a Trump win could bring protectionist policy measures that hurt Chinese exports.

Conclusion

My call in May was based on the big valuation divergence between U.S. and Chinese stocks. The recent surge in Chinese stock indices looks like a sharp bounce from deeply oversold levels. I believe Mr Market's focus on the size of China's stimulus is missing the big picture. The government has drawn a line in the sand and has much more ammunition to support markets. That makes the current valuations still very attractive.

data.title: Tesla's $60 Billion Setback: The Cybercab No-Show
data.description:
The most impressive, and unimpressive, product displays of 2024 occurred last week - from companies headed by the same person. The largest booster rocket ever assembled was called back to terra by gravity; then, as it approached the Earth’s surface, engines ignited that navigated the projectile into a metal cradle.

SpaceX’s 20-story booster rocket catch was nothing short of remarkable. But four days before, analysts were summoned to a product launch that (essentially) didn’t happen.

I know a woman who was engaged to a celebrity chef who, after 300 guests had assembled at a Caribbean resort, was a no-show (no joke) at their wedding. Tesla’s (NASDAQ:TSLA) Cybercab was a no-show at its premiere. Specifically, there were no details that gave any analyst or onlooker much insight into the product or any idea when the rubber will meet the road.

Why was the Cybercab a no-show? In the past two years, CEOs have added trillions to their businesses’ valuations with two letters: AI. Over the last decade, Tesla has sustained its position as the world’s most valuable car company with a one-word claim: autonomous. Tesla’s We, Robot event was a head fake that didn’t fool the market, which promptly trimmed $60 billion from the company’s market cap.

Overvalued

At its peak in 2019, WeWork was valued at $47 billion. Four years later, the co-working company was worth $44 million. In between those two valuations, investors rejected Adam Neumann’s narrative that the business was a tech company and came to see WeWork for what it was — a poorly run real estate play. Tesla is a great company with positive cash flows. However, like Adam Neuman, Musk keeps spewing adjectives and embellishments that have worn thin.

In April, Tesla’s operating profit dropped 50%. This should’ve sent the stock down, but Musk pulled off another mid-air catch. On the earnings call, he said, “We should be thought of as an AI robotics company. If you value Tesla as just an auto company, it’s just the wrong framework.”

Then he added, “If somebody doesn’t believe that Tesla is going to solve autonomy, I think they should not be an investor in the company.” The stock rose 14%. Spoiler alert: Tesla is a car company.

Automotive is Tesla’s core business, accounting for 94% of last year’s revenue. There is a dangerous belief in the U.S. that if you repeat a lie long enough, it will become less of a lie. The election wasn’t stolen, and Tesla is not a software/energy/AI/autonomous firm, but a great car company that is wildly overvalued. (Note: Not financial advice — I consistently get it wrong on Tesla.)

Hybrids

I used to own a Tesla Falcon Model X. It’s a great car from a great brand. Early adopters signaled to the world that they cared about the environment and were rich. Which is a feature that commands margin: being more desirable to potential mates… or less undesirable.

At the moment, however, hybrids appear to be the bridge to a future thought to be electric. Internal combustion autos still account for 81% of new car sales in the U.S., and hybrids are selling 5X faster than EVs in 2024.

Range anxiety and charging concerns are limiting EV sales. One consumer survey found that 80% of those considering an EV for their next car believe the current charging availability is insufficient, while 70% of current EV owners say they’re dissatisfied with the current infrastructure.

That’s bad news for the EV brand, but potentially good news for Tesla, as its charging infrastructure is excellent. However, Musk’s plan to make Tesla charging the industry standard and open its charging stations to all EVs have not materialized.

Another significant factor is price, as hybrids in the U.S., on average, cost $14,884 less than EVs. As EVs attempt to expand beyond early adopters to the mass market, price becomes more important — as the middle of the pyramid is much more price conscious.

For the first time since the pandemic, Tesla saw its sales drop 13% in the first quarter and 9% in the second. Sales rebounded 9% in the third quarter, but the news across the EV sector suggests the category is slowing. Ford (F) is scaling back its EV rollout.

General Motors (GM) is delaying its electric truck and reducing investments in EV battery mining. Volvo (OTCPK:VLVLY) is walking back its 2030 all-electric target. The EV revolution isn’t over, but Toyota (TM) CEO Akio Toyoda was right about its pace. Toyota bet on hybrids, and this year that paid off with a 66% jump in U.S. hybrid sales.

China

In China, though, EVs will account for 45% of new car sales this year. A growing EV pie is good news for a pure-play EV company. But BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF), the No. 1 Chinese EV manufacturer, offers cars at considerably lower prices than Tesla.

The BYD Seagull sells for $9,700 and the Yuan Plus costs $16,600; a Tesla Model 3 starts at $34,000, and the Model Y costs $37,000. China also has 200 domestic EV manufacturers fighting for the EV Iron Throne. The CCP has made clear that it intends to win the EV future via competition.

Mass Distraction

The main attraction at Tesla’s We, Robot event was supposed to be the Cybercab, a two-seat, fully autonomous vehicle that’s long been central to justifying Tesla’s gravity-defying valuation. Unlike the Cybertruck, the Cybercab actually looks cool.

Supposedly, it’ll enter production by 2026, or, as Musk jokes, “before 2027.” The Cybercab will sell for under $30,000, but Musk didn’t offer any details, as financials are a buzzkill. Also, we have no idea whether the self-driving technology will meet regulatory safety standards.

Today, there was news that the main U.S. auto safety regulator said it was investigating Tesla’s self-driving system. Latin for, this tech is not ready for prime-time. But don’t worry. For the past decade, Musk has been predicting that autonomous driving is just around the corner.

Meanwhile, back on Earth, Tesla has only achieved Level 2 autonomy (a driver is still required). The Waymo self-driving taxi, which looks like a science fair project, has Level 4 autonomy (no driver needed), and is already providing 100,000 paid rides per week in Austin, Los Angeles, Phoenix, and San Francisco.

Unlike Tesla, which is trying to go vertical, Waymo wants to be the OS for autonomous driving. The company is working with multiple auto manufacturers, including Fiat Chrysler, Hyundai, and Volvo. My prediction: Waymo’s tech coupled with several brands at different price points will bring autonomous to the masses, not Tesla.

In L.A. last week, I experienced my first Waymo ride. It drives just how I’m hoping my 17-year-old drives, overly cautious. However, when we encountered an accident, with flares, cones, and a cop with a luminant baton, redirecting traffic into the wrong side of the road to pass the damaged vehicle, Waymo was intimidated. But it figured it out, which I found remarkable. The millions of (unexpected) data points collected, digested, and then acted on is staggering.

Glorious Future

Musk believes the Cybercab is going to usher in a “glorious future.” Maybe his claim that entrepreneurs will manage a Cybercab fleet “like a shepherd tends their flock” will become a reality. Maybe a future where Cybercabs are constantly on the move will enable city planners to turn parking lots into parks.

If that day comes, maybe Cathie Wood, who believes Tesla will create $8 trillion in enterprise value by 2029 as a robotaxi business, will look like Warren Buffett. But at the moment she appears to be the Tesla of hedge fund managers, overpromising and underdelivering with inane hyperbole.

Robovan

My Pivot co-host Kara Swisher called the Tesla Robovan a “toaster on wheels.” But Kara also drives a Kia Sorento, which puts an asterisk next to any view she might have on cars.

My take: despite Musk insisting that they will build it, the Robovan is a concept car that has almost no chance of ever being street legal. And Tesla knows this, but autonomous was a no-show at the wedding, and the bride (Musk) needed something to get everyone to look away from the embarrassing situation in front of them.

Peter Drucker advised business leaders to focus on your opportunities, not your problems. And Musk is doing this. SpaceX is ascending, Tesla is falling to Earth. This was obvious at the event, as Musk seemed unrehearsed, confused, and stumbled over his words. BTW, the most impressive person in tech you’ve likely never heard of is Gwynne Shotwell, COO of SpaceX.

Optimus

Musk pitched the Optimus robot as a personal R2-D2 or C-3PO that can be a teacher, babysitter, dog walker, gardener, or companion. “Whatever you can think of, it will do,” Well, I can think of it actually being a robot that has autonomous capabilities. At the event, Optimus robots served as bartenders and chatted with the crowd, which sounds amazing, until you realize that they were controlled by human operators.

Supposedly, the robots will cost somewhere between $20,000 and $30,000 (human operator not included). That’s $12,000 less than a new Tesla Model 3. But a Tesla is a product that solves a real problem — offering personal transportation without burning fossil fuel.

Even if Optimus could do half the things Musk claims it can, who’s going to buy one? Tesla can’t answer that question, but neither can Boston Dynamics. Their Atlas robot requires human oversight, just like Optimus. But after more than a decade of R&D and support from the Defense Department’s DARPA program, Boston Dynamics still doesn’t have a commercial strategy for Atlas, though it has produced some great YouTube videos.

This entire category defines the concept of a technology in search of a use case. However, while it may never be able to watch your kids, Optimus did provide a purposeful distraction from the introduction of a product that is not ready and (once ready) will be inferior to products already in the market.

Pants-on-Fire Mode

Entrepreneur is a synonym for salesperson, and salesperson is the pedestrian term for storyteller. Pro tip: No startup makes sense. We (entrepreneurs) are all impostors who must deploy a fiction (a story) that captures the imagination and attracts capital to pull the future forward and turn rhyme into reason. No business I have started, at the moment of inception, made any sense... until it did. Or didn’t. The only way to predict the future is to make it.

This is not the same as lying. There’s a real distinction between an entrepreneur and a liar: Entrepreneurs believe their story will come true, as they are laser-focused on making it true. A liar, well, they know they’re misleading people with false data. Usually for money (i.e., fraud). This is where Tesla turns gray. Here’s what I see:

  • In 2015, Tesla was two years away from a 1,000-kilometer range... a decade later it’s closer to 600 km.
  • In 2016, coast-to-coast autonomous driving was two years away.
  • In 2019, Tesla said it would have 1 million self-driving cars (that wouldn’t require any human oversight) on the road by mid-2020.
  • Also in 2019, robotaxis were one year away... until last week, when we were told robotaxis were two years away.

I believe this is the year the market looks at Tesla and sees what I see: a great car company that is a car company.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Silver Looks Explosive To The Upside
data.description:
That's the title from the latest issue of my Mining Stock Journal, which I released yesterday. Silver is up nearly 4% as I write this. Here's the title from the September 19th issue: "The Mining Stocks Remain Historically Undervalued." GDX is up 4% as I write this. Several of the micro-cap, project development stocks that I recommend are up over 20% since mid-September. Some of them, like Cabral Gold (OTCPK:CBGZF), have potential 10-20x returns ahead of them. I updated Cabral in yesterday's issue with a couple of imminent catalysts that could trigger a 25% to 35% move in the stock before Christmas.

The left "rim" of the cup goes back to silver's all-time high in 1980. The right "rim" of the cup formed in 2011, when silver peaked just $1 below the 1980 all-time high. The "handle" has been forming over the last 13 years. Perhaps, most interesting, a 5-year chart shows that part of the handle is an upside-down head and shoulders technical formation.

The point here is that silver looks potentially explosive. Since April, silver has been banging its head on $32.50 (Comex front-month contract price basis). In my opinion, if it breaks over $33 and holds, it will trade towards $40 very quickly.

This view is supported by the fundamental set-up in the silver market. It's been well circulated that the Silver Institute of America is forecasting a 215 million ounce silver supply/demand deficit for 2024. Several factors will likely increase the size of that deficit in 2025. First, Russia announced in its Draft Federal Budget release that it plans to significantly increase the holdings in precious metals in its State Fund (sovereign wealth fund). This includes plans to acquire gold, platinum, palladium and, for the first time, silver.

In addition, China recently announced that its build-out of a national solar grid will continue through 2030. This endeavor requires a massive amount of silver. Though China does not publish official silver import numbers, I recall that when the program was underway, it was consuming more than all of the annual amount of silver produced in China. India also has a similar program in place, which is part of the reason it has been importing large amounts of silver. In fact, earlier this week, India announced another $109 billion in grid investments for renewable energy sources. This will also require large quantities of silver.

Finally, at some point - as occurred in the late 1970s and again leading up to the 2011 top in silver - the "poor man's gold" attribute of silver will become a large factor in driving a massive amount of investor money into silver as a cheap substitute for gold. This demand would be coming from the greater public beyond the precious metals "bugs" who have been stacking silver for years.

I believe that in the next 12 to 18 months, silver will make a move higher that will shock just about everyone except the most ardent silver bulls. I don't like to make price forecasts based on a specific point in time, but I think $40 silver within the next year has a high probability. Furthermore, the primary silver producer stocks will soar and the junior silver project development stocks will soar x 5. This is likely why both Coeur Mining (CDE) announced the acquisition of Silvercrest Metals (SILV) and First Majestic (AG) announced the acquisition of Gatos Silver (GATO). Six months to a year from now, the cost to acquire these companies might have doubled.

data.title: C.H. Robinson: Bull Run Could Hit A Wall Following Q3 Earnings
data.description:
CH Robinson Worldwide, Inc. (NASDAQ:CHRW) is about to publish Q3 2024 earnings results on October 30, after market close.

The company has been on the radar of many investors in the integrated freights & logistics sector considering its outperformance in the midst of the current freight recession.

The company managed to surpass earnings expectations in the last two quarters, which led to a 63% increase in the share price.

In my view, there is potential for a further increase, although limited to the $120 price level. I believe so, as the freight market has not fully recovered from its decline in 2022. Therefore, trading at a share price (today) higher than the pre-recession level doesn't make sense to me.

Considering the high earnings expectations in Q3, I believe that any hiccup could lead to a panic selloff. Therefore, the risks outweigh the limited upside.

I will provide my rationale in the outlook section below. For now, I'd like to start with a brief company overview section.

Company Overview

CH Robinson is a global provider of transportation and logistics services, specializing in freight transportation, global forwarding, and additional logistics services like managed services and sourcing through Robinson Fresh.

The company generates revenue by arranging transportation and logistics services for its clients. In other words, they act as intermediaries between carriers and customers, profiting from the margin between what they charge clients and what they pay transportation providers.

I considered including below a breakdown of YTD revenue per business segment and service line from the latest 10-Q.

Ownership-wise, the latest 14A shows 1.18% ownership of common stock among all 18 directors and executive officers. Therefore, I am disappointed that the management team doesn't have much skin in the game.

The Great Freight Recession

Let's start with dessert.

The latest earnings report mentioned a prolonged freight recession. Being skeptical by nature, I checked different sources (here, here, and here) to understand the freight landscape in the US. And, indeed, there is a strong headwind that began in early 2022.

I won't cover in this article the drivers of this recession, as the articles above provide a comprehensive picture of what they call, the Great Freight Recession.

However, I will cover the effects that this recession has on the company. And the main one is the decline in freight rates.

Many carriers and logistics companies, including CHRW, have struggled with reduced margins as they are forced to accept lower rates to stay competitive.

As a matter of fact, approximately 88,000 carriers and 8,000 freight brokers have either shut down or exited the market. Prominent examples include Yellow Corporation, a major less-than-truckload carrier, which filed for bankruptcy in 2023, leaving 30,000 employees jobless, and the collapse of Convoy, a venture-backed digital freight brokerage valued at $3.8 billion.

However, despite this dire picture, freight volumes in North American Surface Transportation (NAST) have shown modest growth.

The company reported a 1.5% increase in less-than-truckload volumes, supported by growth in retail consolidation.

However, I wouldn't pop a bottle of champagne, just yet. While these numbers indicate slight volume increases, they are well below the historical trends and typical seasonal peaks.

Take the Cass Freight Shipment index as an example. In Q2 2024, the index reported a 0.2% sequential increase, compared to a historical average of 6.1% for this quarter.

The reason the historical average is so high is because seasonal freight demand boots volumes during the summer, mainly driven by agricultural demand tied to the produce season.

A direct consequence of this recession is a decline in the truckload line haul rate per mile, which decreased in Q2 2024 by 2% YoY. This pricing pressure has led to a 2.9% decline in total NAST revenues, falling from $3.08 billion in Q2 2023 to $2.99 billion in Q2 2024.

However, despite this decline, the company managed to increase by 6.5% its adjusted gross profit per truckload shipment. Additionally, less-than-truckload services experienced a 5.0% increase in adjusted gross profit per order.

Bozeman reiterated during the earnings call that while the company is making progress, it expects the freight market recovery to remain slow, emphasizing that the company is focusing on operational efficiency rather than relying on an imminent market rebound.

Michael Castagnetto, President of North American Surface Transportation, highlighted the uncertainty of when a meaningful market rebound will occur. I included below an excerpt

Within Q2, we saw some seasonal volume strength in June, primarily related to produce season, but overall seasonality was muted, as shown in the Cass Freight Shipment Index increasing only 0.2% sequentially versus the 10-year average of a 6.1% sequential increase.

Outlook

I'd like to start with price action.

The daily chart below shows a 43% decline in the share price between the start of the freight recession and May this year.

However, notice the 62% jump between May and mid-October. The turnaround was initiated on the exact day of Q1 2024 earnings release. So, what happened then?

Well, in my view, the turnaround was driven by the announcement that the North American Surface Transportation truckload and less-than-truckload volumes increased by 1.5% compared to the same period in 2023.

Additionally, sequential improvements were noted within the quarter, which likely signaled to investors that demand was stabilizing and possibly rebounding.

The shareholder excitement drove the share price up, possibly as investors believed that the company was excelling during the recession headwinds.

The chart below perfectly reflects the performance of the company during the freight recession. Notice the decline in the freight and logistics sector since 2022.

So, the big question: does it make sense to buy now?

Well, my answer is no, as I believe the risks outweigh the upside.

Particularly, I believe there is a high expectation for the company to beat earnings and show revenue growth.

Additionally, as seen above, the EPS expectation is high. Any deviation from this expectation could lead to a cool-off in shareholder sentiment, potentially leading to a panic selloff.

Will it happen? I don't know.

But if it does, and depending on the magnitude of the selloff, I believe that could be a good opportunity to open a long position in the stock.

However, today, I believe that the risk of a panic selloff is too high to justify opening a long position before Q3 2024 earnings day.

Additionally, the recent bull run has led to high valuation ratios, most of them trading at their 2-year max.

Looking at the financials, the income statement looks favorable in Q2 2024, with a positive increase in revenue YoY since Q2 2022. The improvement was also visible in operating and net income, with a double-digit growth in the last quarter.

However, the cash flow statement shows a 26% decline in operating cash flow in the last quarter. Among the drivers for this decline was a significant increase in receivables of $290 million in 2024 compared to a decrease of $501 million in 2023.

It seems that more revenue was recognized without corresponding cash collection, which is not a particularly positive sign.

Looking at the company's liquidity ratios, I am concerned about the low cash ratio of 0.05.

Ratio Value
Current Ratio 1.42
Quick Ratio 1.10
Cash Ratio 0.05

A closer look at the balance statement shows a sharp decline in cash per share. However, the net debt of the company has remained relatively stable since Q2 2024, declining by 1% YoY.

It looks like the company used its cash reserves to pay cash dividends, amounting to $147.3 million in H1 2024, and for repayments on short-term borrowings.

The repayments on short-term borrowings added up to $1.625 billion in H1 2024. However, they received $1.653 billion in proceeds from short-term borrowings during this period.

Therefore, it seems that the company has been using cash reserves for dividend payments.

In fact, they just raised their dividend to $0.62, marking the first increase since December 2022.

The activist activity doesn't look favorable either, with a 16.5% reduction in ownership by State Street Corp just a few days ago, on October 16.

However, in Q2 2024, there was a decent increase of 125% in new positions by institutional investors. Additionally, the put/call ratio was down by 28%.

However, since Q2, the positive sentiment among institutional investors seems to have cooled off, with major players, like Morgan Stanley, selling 312k shares in Q3 2024.

So far, the latest big purchase was made by Point72 Asset Management in Q2 2024, with 787k shares purchased at an estimated average price (according to Whale Wisdom) of $78.

Finally, the last reason for my Hold rating is the high valuation ratios of the company compared to its competitors. The table below is sorted in descending order by P/B value.

Conclusion

In conclusion, I am maintaining a Hold rating, as I believe the potential upside before the Q3 2024 earnings release does not outweigh the downside risks.

Considering that the headwinds in the freight and logistics industry, and the high EPS expectations in the next earnings release, coupled with high shareholder excitement following great Q1 and Q2 results, I believe that missing earnings in the midst of the freight recession could drive the share price down in a panic selloff. If this is the case, and I am not betting that it will happen, then a lower share price could make the valuation ratios more attractive, potentially being a good entry point for a long position in the company.

I remain concerned about the 26% drop in operating cash flow and its low cash ratio. This, combined with an increase in receivables without corresponding cash collection, could suggest potential liquidity pressures.

Additionally, the insider selling activity in August, and the recent sales by major institutional investors, lowers my conviction in the current share price.

To wrap up, despite revenue and profitability growth in the last quarter and some early signs that the freight recession is starting to wind down, I rate CH Robinson as a Hold. I recommend keeping a close eye on Q3 earnings results and management's commentary regarding the evolution of the freight recession.

data.title: U.S. IPO Weekly Recap: Small Issuers Keep The October IPO Market Moving
data.description:
Four very small Asian issuers listed in the US this past week. The offerings raised just $26 million combined.

The week's big news came from the pipeline, with China-based autonomous driving play Pony AI (PONY) filing for a $300 million US IPO. The company currently operates a fleet of over 250 robotaxis, licensed to operate in all four Tier-1 cities in China, and 190 robotrucks.

Hong Kong printer Samfine Creation Holdings Group (SFHG) priced at the low end to raise $8 million at an $80 million market cap. The company traded up on its debut and jumped further in the aftermarket, finishing the week with a 124% return from offer. Another Chinese name, tea producer Oriental Rise Holdings (ORIS) raised $7 million at an $87 million market cap. It also traded well, ending up 94%.

Two Singapore companies completed US IPOs, both of which priced their offerings at the low end. Construction firm Springview Holdings (SPHL) raised $6 million at an $86 million market cap, and fuel logistics provider PTL (PTLE) raised $5 million at a $50 million market cap. Both finished with modest gains.

data.title: Better Growth Prospects Make EPR Properties Attractive Despite Higher Valuation
data.description:
I've strongly advocated for EPR Properties (NYSE:EPR) high upside potential since my activity in Seeking Alpha began. In fact, my first coverage was about EPR. The Company holds one of the most prominent positions in my REIT portfolio. Since the last time I covered EPR, its stock price has increased by 11.7%, constituting a 15.4% total return. Such a dynamic increase left many investors wondering whether EPR is still worth investing in. Let me bring you some good news that may ease your doubts, and if you are interested in the development of my views on EPR or a more detailed review of their struggles and recovery within the theatre segment - please refer to the article linked above.

As I expected, EPR's cost of capital improved

REIT investors have to realize how REITs grow. There are two primary growth drivers, one being internal and the other external:

  1. Internally-drive growth relates to increasing rent on owned properties, securing more favourable lease agreements, and improving occupancy
  2. Externally-driven growth relates to gathering capital (either through equity issues, debt financing, property disposition proceeds, or retained cash flow) and allocating it to investment opportunities

Let's concentrate on external growth, which involves two sides of the equation:

  • Cost of capital used to finance investment activity
  • Investment yield measured by cap rates (annualized rent from a given property divided by its purchase price)

To ensure growth and create value for shareholders, REITs have to realize positive spreads on their investments, so in simple terms, gather higher cap rates than the cost of capital they used to finance investments.

As EPR's stock price has increased dynamically, its cost of equity improved (lowered), as the Company is capable of gathering more capital through issuing a given amount of shares. Therefore, such an operation is more accretive.

The last time I covered EPR, its investment spread (disregarding retained cash flow and disposition proceeds) was below 0%, painting a negative picture of its investment activity. Let's take another try to calculate it by assuming:

  • cost of equity as EPR's AFFO yield
  • cost of debt based on EPR's credit rating
  • market capital structure

Please review the table below for details.

Moreover, let's keep in mind that EPR retains some cash flow and realizes disposition proceeds, ensuring the Company has some 'internally generated' capital to finance its investments. If we account for its AFFO adjusted (in minus) by its dividend distributions and (in plus) disposition proceeds, we could assume that EPR financed ~78% of its Q1-Q2 2024 investments through retained cash flow.

Financing bodies trust the turnaround story

EPR Properties has recently announced that it entered a new $1B revolving credit facility agreement, replacing its previous one (for the same amount) but bringing some more attractive terms into the table as the new contract:

  • reduced the interest rate costs
  • doesn't include the tangible worth covenant
  • allows EPR to enter into another secured debt agreement
  • simplified the valuation method of related assets

Regarding the term, the facility matures in October 2028 and includes two 6-month extension options (upon additional fees). As stated in the press release, the proceeds are to be used not only for general business purposes but also to finance investment activity, suggesting that EPR will have some acquisitions to showcase in the upcoming quarters:

The Company expects to use borrowings under the new facility for general business purposes, including the acquisition of experiential properties consistent with its current strategy. “We are pleased to announce the completion of this new credit facility, which provides us with enhanced borrowing flexibility and more favorable terms,” stated Greg Silvers, President and CEO of EPR Properties.

It's important to recognize that the consent to grant EPR another line of financing at more favorable terms expresses the improved perspective that the financing institution has on the company. For me, that's another signal that EPR is continuing to progress on its turnaround story.

Valuation Outlook

Being an M&A advisor (fancy name for advising on buying and selling businesses), I usually rely on a multiple valuation method that is a leading tool in transaction processes, as it allows for accessible and market-driven benchmarking within a specific peer group.

With that said, the forward-looking P/FFO multiple stood at:

  • 10.0x for EPR Properties
  • 13.4x for Gaming and Leisure Properties (GLPI)
  • 12.7x for VICI Properties (VICI)
  • 18.5x for Agree Realty Corporation (ADC)
  • 17.9x for Essential Properties Realty Trust (EPRT)
  • 14.7x for NNN REIT (NNN)

EPR is still cheap, even after recent stock price raises. I believe its multiple valuation will continue to expand as the Company has been recovering dynamically from post-COVID issues, evident in business updates provided in recent quarters and continued payments of well-covered dividends. On October 15, 2024, EPR declared another monthly dividend of $0.285 per share, payable in November. EPR's forward-looking AFFO payout ratio stands at ~71%, which is more than reasonable and safe considering investors' negative sentiment.

Regarding the multiple, I consider another 5-10% appreciation within EPR's range.

Investment Thesis and Risk Factors

Even after substantial stock price increases, EPR remains an attractively valued (frankly, undervalued) REIT opportunity. The Company is set to witness further multiple appreciation. On the distribution front, its dividends are safe and well-supported by a relatively low AFFO payout ratio.

Although the upside potential isn't as significant as it used to be, it's still an attractive risk-to-reward ratio. I reduced my previously assigned rating from 'strong buy' to 'buy' in the face of less substantial upside potential; however, positive developments on the financing and cost of capital front (which led me to expect positive results in the upcoming quarterly release) have me to remain bullish on EPR. I am happy to uphold it as one of my key holdings in the REIT space.

Nevertheless, each stock market investment is accompanied by market and company-specific risk factors, which in the case of EPR include:

  • still relatively high cost of capital, limiting investment activity
  • ongoing process of portfolio reorganization
  • stock price volatility in the face of any tenant-related press releases, which wouldn't necessarily imply any meaningful problems
  • uncertainty regarding the interest rate environment and the economy
